---
title: "Chapter 4: Placer-Au prospectivity (northern Sierra)"
subtitle: "Two-population (Tertiary deep-gravel + Quaternary modern-channel) at 250 m on the canonical California placer province"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## What this chapter is about

Placer-Au prospectivity modeling sits about a decade behind lode in the
published literature. The lode side has stacked supervised pipelines with
spatial-block cross-validation, leakage audits, and external blind tests
as standard practice. The placer side mostly does not, because the data
is messier (point occurrences with inconsistent `dep_type` strings), the
positives concentrate in a few well-known districts, and the geomorphic
chain from lode source to placer trap involves transport mechanics that
do not show up in a static feature stack. The only production-grade
transferable placer model that survives in the published record is the
USGS Alaska watershed scorer used in the Cross, Frost, and Karl
assessments (USGS Open-File Report series), a weighted-sum
knowledge-driven index rather than a supervised classifier. It works
because Alaska placer-bearing watersheds are large and the scoring
features are tractable per-watershed averages.

This chapter ports that knowledge-driven approach to the northern Sierra
deep-gravel belt at 250 m grid resolution, adds Sierra-specific features
that capture the deep-gravel geometry (terraces above the modern
drainage, not channel bottoms), then runs a Phase 2 supervised pass
(PU bagging + Random Forest + LightGBM + stacking + isotonic
calibration) with the same discipline the lode side already practices.
The result is two calibrated rasters, one per geomorphic placer
population, fused per-cell into a single deliverable GeoTIFF that the
[goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) live tool samples for
per-claim prospectivity scoring.

Two populations of placer with different geomorphic signatures:

- **Tertiary deep-gravel**, the auriferous-gravel paleo-channels that
  the ancestral Yuba and American rivers cut before late-Tertiary uplift,
  later buried by andesitic flows, then re-exposed by hydraulic mining
  in the 1850s-1884 era. Pay strata sit on hillside benches above the
  modern drainage. Positive labels: the 167 Orlando 2016 hydraulic-mine
  pit polygons (158 unique cells on the 250 m grid).
- **Quaternary modern-channel**, active stream alluvium along the
  modern Yuba / Bear / American river system and their tributaries.
  Positive labels: 573 cells from MRDS records with `dep_type` matching
  placer / alluvial / stream / gravel.

The Phase 1 weighted-knowledge-driven index runs across both populations
with a single composite score; Phase 2 trains one classifier per
population and fuses the calibrated rasters with `np.maximum`.

## Phase 1: knowledge-driven anchor gate

The Phase 1 scorer combines eleven features into a p1/p99-clipped
min-max-normalized weighted sum, including `distance_to_lode_m` (omnidirectional Euclidean
to nearest MRDS lode-Au seed), `paleochannel_likelihood` (a composite of
NHDPlus HR-derived REM + local relief + geomorphon-based GMI),
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m`, `tertiary_terrace_likelihood` (a
geometric-mean composite of TPI-high, slope-low, not-Qal that scores the
bench-above-drainage geometry directly), and `catchment_au_hawkes` (Hawkes
1976 dual-decay catchment aggregation of NGDB + NURE stream-sediment Au).
The full feature stack and per-layer maps are documented in the
[data exploration notebook](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/data_exploration.html).

The Phase 1 validation gate: seven historic deep-gravel anchor districts
(Malakoff Diggins, North San Juan, Dutch Flat, You Bet, Iowa Hill,
Forest Hill, Michigan Bluff) must each land in the top decile of the
score. None of the seven appears in the training labels (the matching
hydraulic-pit polygons are excluded by name). The v1 model gets
**7 of 7**. A held-out Lindgren PP73 secondary set of thirteen
non-anchor Tertiary districts (Smartsville, Camptonville, Alleghany,
Damascus, Lake City, Sucker Flat, French Corral, Sweetland, Cherokee,
Indian Hill, Brandy City, Snake Creek, Quartz Mountain) lands at median
decile 0 (same as the anchors), 11 of 13 in the top quintile. The
feature engineering generalizes; the gate is not an anchor-fit
artifact.

## Phase 2: supervised, per population

The v2 pipeline trains one classifier stack per population on the same
250 m feature frame:

1. **PU bagging** (Mordelet-Vert positive-unlabeled bagging,
   `n_bags=30`) as the one-class baseline that does not assume the
   unlabeled set is negative.
2. **Random Forest** and **LightGBM** under 20 km spatial-block
   cross-validation, with per-fold Hawkes catchment-geochem recompute so
   a test-fold sample never contributes to a feature the model sees at
   fit time.
3. **Stacking meta-learner**: logistic regression over the spatial-block
   out-of-fold predictions from RF and LightGBM. The meta uses the same
   20 km cross-validation iterable, so it never sees in-block test
   positives during fit.
4. **Isotonic calibration** via `CalibratedClassifierCV`. Platt sigmoid
   fallback when a population has fewer than 30 positives.
5. **Per-population fusion**: the two calibrated rasters combine
   per-cell via `np.maximum` into a single deliverable GeoTIFF.

Every population mask, fold split, and calibration fold excludes the
seven anchor districts, so the validation gate stays a real held-out
test.

## Tertiary v2 cross-validation by model

![Per-fold spatial-block ROC-AUC for the three Tertiary models. Each blue or brown dot is one of 14 spatial blocks; the black bar is the model's mean across folds. Stacking is plotted as the single global out-of-fold score because the meta-learner is trained once on the OOF base scores from RF + LightGBM.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v2_fold_auc_tertiary.png)

What the chart is telling you. RF averages 0.995 across 14 spatial
blocks with std 0.008. That number is too good. It is the model
memorizing the spatial neighborhood around each Tertiary positive: the
positive labels are hydraulic-pit polygon centroids, and
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` is in the feature set. Held-out blocks still
contain the zero-distance cells that ring each positive; RF (deep
trees, no regularization, no shrinkage on small leaves) trivially
identifies them. The feature is not leaking across folds in the
strict-data-leakage sense, but it is structurally inverse-encoding the
label, which the model picks up.

LightGBM averages 0.804 across the same 14 folds with std 0.101,
including two folds at 0.5. The regularized boosting framework resists
the pit-proximity memorization but pays for it with fold-to-fold
variance. The two folds at 0.5 are blocks where the held-out positives
have no nearby pit proximity signal in the training data.

Stacking averages the two via logistic regression on the spatial-block
OOF predictions, reaching a global OOF AUC of 0.961 and a PR-AUC of
0.116 (the latter low because the 158 positives among 800k cells is a
0.02% prior, so any model with non-trivial recall produces low
precision-weighted area). The meta-learner down-weights the
RF score where it disagrees with LightGBM, pulling the headline number
back to a range the per-fold LightGBM evidence actually supports.

## What the calibrated Tertiary raster looks like

![Tertiary v2 calibrated probability, displayed as percentile rank across the AOI (the calibrated probability has a long zero tail that linear color stretching would wash out). The cyan outlines are the 167 Orlando 2016 hydraulic-mine pit polygons. The red dots are the seven anchor districts, none of which appear in the training labels.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v2_calibrated_tertiary_map.png)

The hot zone (yellow / orange) traces the Mother Lode trend through the
western part of the AOI; the eastern half (toward the Sierra crest) is
mostly zero-probability black. The anchor districts all land in the hot
zone. The eastern dark zone is correct: the Mother Lode lode-Au sources
are concentrated west, and the geomorphic features that score Tertiary
placers (paleo-channel REM, terrace TPI, pit proximity, low slope) all
fade east of the lode trend.

## Tertiary v2 anchor + held-out validation

![Decile distribution of the calibrated Tertiary probability for three point sets: the seven anchor districts (trained out of every fold; red), the thirteen Lindgren PP73 secondary Tertiary districts (held out entirely; blue), and the five Lindgren PP73 secondary Quaternary districts (held out entirely; orange). v2 is Tertiary-specific, so Lindgren Quaternary correctly clusters in the bottom decile.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v2_decile_histogram_tertiary.png)

The anchor districts split 6 in the top quintile (3 at d0, 3 at d1)
plus 1 in the bottom decile. That single d9 anchor is Forest Hill,
which the Phase 1 gate also placed at the bottom of the passing set
(decile 2 there). Forest Hill is geologically the least pit-proximal of
the seven, and the v2 isotonic calibrator pushes it into the zero bin
because no nearby positive cells anchor its probability.

The Lindgren Tertiary set splits cleanly: 5 cells in d1 (matching the
anchor cluster), 8 cells in d9 (the zero bin). The calibration produces
a bimodal output by design: cells are either "near a pit-proximity
signal" or "not", with little middle ground. Eight Lindgren districts
landing in the zero bin is the model saying it does not have enough
local evidence to rank them above the floor, not that they are not
placer-prospective. The Phase 1 weighted-knowledge-driven score still
ranks 11 of 13 in the top quintile because it uses the full feature
stack at low resolution, not the calibrated supervised output.

The Lindgren Quaternary set sits in the zero bin almost entirely
(4 of 5 at d9, 1 at d1), which is what we want from a Tertiary-specific
classifier. The Quaternary classifier (v2 Quaternary pipeline, in
training as this chapter is being written) will handle them.

## What v2 buys over Phase 1

The Phase 1 weighted index uses a fixed linear combination of features
that the geologist set by hand. The v2 supervised pass replaces the
hand-tuning with stacked tree models that learn the feature interactions
from the labels themselves. Two concrete improvements:

1. **Per-population specialization**. Phase 1 was Tertiary-tuned because
   all seven training anchors are Tertiary. v2 trains a separate
   classifier per population, so the Quaternary branch can weight
   stream-power, catchment-Au Hawkes, and is_quaternary_alluvium
   appropriately on the 573 modern-channel positives.
2. **Calibration to a real probability scale**. Phase 1 produces a
   weighted-sum index in [0, 1] that is not a calibrated probability
   (cells at 0.85 are not "85% likely to host a placer"). v2's isotonic
   calibration step maps the stacked score to a probability that can be
   thresholded and explained to a non-modeler.

The Phase 1 raster stays useful as a feature-rich knowledge-driven prior
that complements v2; the v2 calibrated raster is the deliverable that
the goldbug tool ingests.

## What this does not prove

This is a regional prospectivity model trained on 158 + 573 positive
cells over an 805,000-cell grid. It says where the model places its
probability mass given the public-data feature stack and the spatial
distribution of the labels. It does not say:

- **The hot cells contain extractable gold.** They contain features
  that match the trained labels' features. Hydraulic-pit polygons mark
  where the 1850s-1884 miners chose to wash hillsides; they do not
  prove every ranked-high cell has commercial pay.
- **The model transfers off the AOI.** A Calaveras transfer test
  (Phase J.3) is queued; expect a Klamath-style drop (Mother Lode →
  Klamath went from AUC 0.85 to 0.58 on the lode side). Within-AOI
  generalization is the story so far; out-of-AOI is the next test.
- **The Phase 1 anchor gate is a perfect external test.** The Lindgren
  PP73 secondary set is the better external test: 13 Tertiary districts
  never seen by any classifier or scorer. v2 catches 5 of 13 in the
  high-percentile zone; the rest land in the bimodal zero bin.
- **The Tertiary RF AUC of 0.995 is the headline number.** The stacking
  meta-learner's 0.961 is closer to what the model will achieve on new
  cells. The RF number is what the model can do when it is allowed to
  memorize the label-inverse feature.

## Hand-off to goldbug

The deliverable raster
`prospectivity_placer_placer_tertiary_250m_calibrated_4326.tif` (plus
the matching `_3310.tif` in California Albers, and the same pair for
Quaternary when the v2 Quaternary pipeline finishes) lands in
`data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/`. The
[goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) sibling repo samples the
fused raster with its existing zonal-mean machinery: per-claim mean +
max + count + has-data flag against the calibrated probability,
weighted by the claim polygon footprint. No API contract beyond the
GeoTIFF; the goldbug consumer treats the raster as one more input
layer.

## Deeper notebooks

- [Data exploration: feature stack walkthrough](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/data_exploration.html): every input layer (DEM, slope, TPI, NHD hydrology, hydraulic pits, CGS geology, MRDS lode seeds, paleochannel composite, Tertiary terrace likelihood, NGDB + NURE catchment Hawkes) with a map at each step, ending at the Phase 1 anchor + Lindgren validation result.
- [Northern Sierra placer integrated report](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.html): the polished writeup with methodology details, reproduction commands, and the v2 audit checklist.
- [Internal review notes](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/internal.html): per-fold metric tables, leakage audit traces, decision log, and the v3 Phase candidates list.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
